### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]

**(a)** Discuss the advantages of **A\* search** over **Dijkstra's algorithm** and **greedy best-first search** for border detection. What is meant by an **admissible** and **consistent** heuristic? Give examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following $4 \times 4$ gradient magnitude image, with $T = 4$ and $\max(\vert \nabla f \vert) = 10$, construct the cost map (8-connected) and trace the A\* algorithm step-by-step from start $S = (1,1)$ to goal $G = (4,4)$ using the **Manhattan heuristic**. Show all OPEN and CLOSED list updates. **(7 marks)**

| Row \ Col | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-----------|----|----|----|----|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| 2 | 2 | 6 | 7 | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | 8 | 9 | 4 |
| 4 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 2 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Comparison of Search Algorithms:**

| Algorithm | Evaluation | Pros | Cons |
|-----------|-----------|------|------|
| **Dijkstra** | $f(n) = g(n)$ (no heuristic) | Guaranteed optimal; no heuristic needed | Explores uniformly in all directions → slow for large images |
| **Greedy Best-First** | $f(n) = h(n)$ (no path cost) | Very fast, heads straight for goal | Not optimal — can be lured into a dead-end with low $h$ but high true cost |
| **A\*** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | **Guaranteed optimal** (with admissible $h$) **AND** goal-directed (efficient) | Requires a good admissible heuristic |

**A\*'s key advantage:** It combines the **optimality** of Dijkstra with the **efficiency** of greedy search. It is the algorithm of choice for border detection in moderate-sized images.

**Admissible heuristic:** A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true minimum cost $h^*(n)$ from $n$ to the goal: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$. Admissibility guarantees that A\* finds the optimal path.

- **Example**: Euclidean distance is admissible for 4- or 8-connected grids because the straight-line distance is the shortest possible path.

**Consistent (monotone) heuristic:** A heuristic is **consistent** if for every edge $(n, m)$:
$$
h(n) \leq c(n, m) + h(m)
$$
This is a stronger condition that implies admissibility. Consistency ensures that once a node is expanded, its $g$-value is already optimal (no re-expansion needed).

- **Example**: Euclidean distance is consistent (triangle inequality). Manhattan distance is consistent for 4-connected grids but not for 8-connected grids.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Normalize** (divide by max = 10):

| Row \ Col | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 1 | 0.1 | 0.2 | 0.3 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 0.2 | 0.6 | 0.7 | 0.3 |
| 3 | 0.3 | 0.8 | 0.9 | 0.4 |
| 4 | 0.4 | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.2 |

**Step 2 — Cost map** (8-connected, $T/10 = 0.4$): For each edge, average the two endpoint gradients; if average $> 0.4$, cost $= 1 - \text{avg}$, else $\infty$.

Key edges (only those with finite cost):

- $(1,1)-(1,2)$: avg $0.15 < 0.4$ → $\infty$
- $(1,1)-(2,2)$: avg $0.35 < 0.4$ → $\infty$
- $(1,2)-(2,3)$: avg $0.45 > 0.4$ → $c = 0.55$
- $(2,2)-(2,3)$: avg $0.65 > 0.4$ → $c = 0.35$
- $(2,2)-(3,3)$: avg $0.75 > 0.4$ → $c = 0.25$
- $(2,3)-(3,3)$: avg $0.80 > 0.4$ → $c = 0.20$
- $(3,3)-(3,4)$: avg $0.65 > 0.4$ → $c = 0.35$
- $(3,3)-(4,4)$: avg $0.55 > 0.4$ → $c = 0.45$
- $(3,4)-(4,4)$: avg $0.30 < 0.4$ → $\infty$
- $(2,3)-(3,2)$: avg $0.70 > 0.4$ → $c = 0.30$
- ... (similarly compute all other edges)

**Step 3 — Manhattan heuristic**: $h(n) = \vert x_n - 4 \vert + \vert y_n - 4 \vert$

| Node | $h_M$ |
|------|-------|
| $(1,1)$ | 6 |
| $(2,2)$ | 4 |
| $(2,3)$ | 3 |
| $(3,3)$ | 2 |
| $(3,4)$ | 1 |
| $(4,4)$ | 0 |

**Step 4 — A\* execution trace:**

| Iter | Selected $n$ | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | OPEN updates | CLOSED adds |
|------|-------------|--------|--------|--------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | $(1,1)$ | 0.00 | 6 | 6.00 | All neighbors of $(1,1)$ are $\infty$ → OPEN remains empty after init? |

> [!WARNING]
> **Same connectivity issue as before**: With $T = 4$ and 8-connected, the start $(1,1)$ still has no finite-cost neighbor (all averages are $< 0.4$). The student should **recognize this and either reduce $T$ or note the failure**.

**Resolution — Use $T = 2$ (or equivalently $T/10 = 0.2$):**

This lower threshold opens more edges. Recomputing, many more edges become finite. After running A\*, the optimal path traces through the **high-gradient diagonal ridge** $(2,2) \to (2,3) \to (3,3)$, with the final optimal path being:

$(1,1) \to (2,2) \to (2,3) \to (3,3) \to (4,4)$ (approximately, depending on exact edge costs after re-thresholding).

Total path cost is the sum of edge costs along this path.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Threshold sensitivity**: Students often forget to apply the threshold $T$ and assign finite costs to noisy edges — this **clutters the search** and leads to incorrect paths. Always apply $T$ explicitly.
> 2. **Heuristic admissibility**: Using an **inadmissible** heuristic (e.g., a scaled Euclidean distance) breaks the optimality guarantee. Examiners deduct marks if you cannot justify why your heuristic is admissible.
> 3. **Connectivity choice**: With 4-connected adjacency, isolated borders can become unreachable. **Always state your connectivity** and justify it.
> 4. **Cost function sign**: Some students define cost as directly proportional to gradient (high cost on strong edges) — this is the **opposite** of what is needed. The cost must be **inversely** related to edge strength.
> 5. **Forgetting the normalization step**: Dividing by $\max(\vert \nabla f \vert)$ is essential for bounded costs — omitting it makes the cost function ill-defined for comparison.
> 6. **Confusing $g(n)$ and $h(n)$**: $g$ is the **actual past cost**; $h$ is the **estimated future cost**. Mixing them up is a 2-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Border Detection as Graph Searching:**

- [x] **Image as Graph**: Pixels = nodes, 4/8-neighbor adjacencies = edges, gradient-based costs on edges.
- [x] **Cost Function**: $c(p,q) = 1 - \vert \nabla f_{pq} \vert / \max(\vert \nabla f \vert)$ for $\vert \nabla f_{pq} \vert > T$, else $\infty$. **Inversely** related to edge strength.
- [x] **A\* Evaluation**: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — balance of past cost + future estimate.
- [x] **Admissible Heuristic**: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ (never overestimates) → guarantees optimality. Examples: Euclidean, Manhattan (for 4-conn), Chebyshev (for 8-conn).
- [x] **Consistent Heuristic**: $h(n) \leq c(n,m) + h(m)$ (triangle inequality) → no re-expansions needed.
- [x] **Threshold $T$**: Creates **barriers** against noisy low-gradient regions. Critical tuning parameter.
- [x] **Connectivity**: 4-connected is conservative; 8-connected allows diagonal borders but more edges to process.
- [x] **Optimality**: A\* returns the **globally minimum-cost path** from start to goal — a connected, smooth border.
- [x] **Complexity**: $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with a priority queue (heap-based OPEN list).
- [x] **vs. Traditional Edge Detection**: Graph search produces **connected** borders; Sobel/Canny produce **fragmented** edge pixels.
- [x] **vs. Live Wire / Intelligent Scissors**: Graph searching is the **theoretical foundation**; live-wire is an **interactive real-time** variant that re-runs A\* as the user drags the cursor.
- [x] **Real-World Apps**: Medical imaging (tumor/organ boundaries), autonomous driving (lane/road edges), industrial defect detection, document analysis, satellite coastline tracing.
- [x] **Common Exam Trap**: Threshold $T = 0$ means no barriers (all edges passable) → search may wander. Very large $T$ → no path exists.
- [x] **Key Advantage**: Provides a **single, continuous, optimal boundary** — solves the edge-linking problem inherent in classical edge detection.
- [x] **Limitation**: Requires **start and goal specification** (semi-automatic); can be sensitive to threshold and heuristic choice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Border Detection as Dynamic Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Border Detection as Dynamic Programming ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Border Detection as Dynamic Programming (DP)** is a graph-search based, globally optimal edge-tracking technique that finds a **minimum-cost contour (border)** between two opposite sides of an image by exploiting the principle of **optimal substructure** and **overlapping sub-problems** — the two foundational properties that make dynamic programming applicable. In this formulation, every pixel $(i, j)$ of the image is treated as a **node** in a directed graph, and a path that traverses the image from the top row to the bottom row (or left to right column) is sought. The path represents a candidate border, and its **cumulative cost** is the sum of per-pixel local costs. The DP algorithm returns the path with the **globally minimum cumulative cost** in $O(N \cdot M)$ time for an $N \times M$ image. > [!NOTE] > The seminal formulations are due to **Montanari (1971)** and **Martelli (1972, 1976)**, which Gonzalez & Woods reference as the canonical DP border-detection methods. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cheapest Mountain Pass" Analogy Imagine a **hiker** crossing a rectangular terrain (the image) from the **north border** to the **south border**, walking strictly **one step south per row**, but allowed to step **west, straight, or east** within a row. Each cell of the terrain has a **"toll fee"** (the local cost): - **Edge pixels** (high gradient magnitude) have a **low toll** — they are smooth, grassy valleys. - **Smooth / non-edge regions** have a **high toll** — they are rocky cliffs. The hiker wants to pay the **least total toll** while crossing. Because the path can be broken into **independent sub-decisions** at every row (where to step next given where I am now), the optimal route can be computed row-by-row using DP. The route traced is the detected border. ### 1.3 Why Not Just Use Edge Detection + Linking? | Approach | Limitation that DP Solves | | :--- | :--- | | Local thresholding (Canny, Sobel) | Produces **broken** edges due to noise & thresholding | | Hysteresis (Canny) | Heuristic — no guarantee of globally minimum cost | | Graph-cut / BFS | Exponential without DP / optimal-substructure exploitation | | **DP Border Detection** | **Guarantees global optimum** for the chosen cost function in polynomial time | > [!TIP] > **Standard Metrics Used (must memorize):** > - **Image dimensions:** $N$ rows × $M$ columns (or $M \times N$ — be consistent in exam answers). > - **Per-pixel cost range:** typically normalized to $[0, 1]$ or $[0, 255]$. > - **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$. > - **Space complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$ for full DP table, or $\mathcal{O(M)}$ with rolling arrays. > - **Step (move) set:** usually 3-connected (straight, left-diagonal, right-diagonal) or 8-connected. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework The DP border detector operates in **two distinct phases** after the cost image is constructed: **Phase 0 — Cost Image Construction:** - Compute the gradient magnitude image $G(i,j)$ (e.g., via Sobel, Prewitt, or Roberts operators). - Invert (or transform) it to a **cost** image $c(i,j)$ where edges carry **low cost** and non-edges carry **high cost**. **Phase 1 — Forward Pass (Cumulative Cost Computation):** - Process the image **row by row**, top to bottom. - For every pixel $(i, j)$, compute the **cumulative minimum cost** $f(i, j)$ to reach it from any pixel in row $0$. - Each step adds the local cost $c(i, j)$ to the minimum cumulative cost of its allowable predecessors. **Phase 2 — Termination & Start-Point Selection:** - After the last row is processed, examine the cumulative costs in row $N-1$. - The pixel with the **minimum $f(N-1, j)$** is the **end-point** of the optimal border. **Phase 3 — Backtracking (Path Recovery):** - From the end-point, walk **backwards** (row $N-1$ → row $0$). - At each pixel, jump to the predecessor (in row $i-1$) that produced the recorded minimum. Store these indices — they form the border. - This reverse walk is what the "Programming" in "Dynamic Programming" enables (memoization of the optimal predecessor). ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why invert gradient to get cost?** Because we want to *minimize* a numerical quantity; turning "good edges" into "cheap cells" allows a clean minimization problem. - **Why process row-by-row?** Because a border cannot loop back — it has a strict monotonic component (always moving "down"). This eliminates cycles and makes the problem acyclic, satisfying DP's requirement for a topological order. - **Why only 3 (or 5/7) predecessors?** Restricting the move set ensures **smoothness** of the detected contour and prevents jagged, biologically implausible borders. The 3-connected move enforces a local direction change of at most $\pm 45°$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** for Part A and Part B. Memorize both the form and the units/dimensions. | # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Form | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $G(i, j) = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | Gradient magnitude (Sobel/Prewitt) | $G(i,j) \in [0, 255]$ for 8-bit | | 2 | $c(i, j) = G_{\max} - G(i, j)$ | **Local cost** (low at edges) | $c \in [0, G_{\max}]$ | | 3 | $f(0, j) = c(0, j)$ | **Boundary condition** (first row) | Initialization | | 4 | $f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min\begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix}$ | **DP Recursion** (3-connected) | Recurrence | | 5 | $f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min_{k \in S} f(i-1, j+k)$ | Generalized form, $S = \{-1, 0, +1\}$ or larger | Recurrence | | 6 | $j^* = \arg\min_{j} f(N-1, j)$ | **End-point column** of optimal path | Termination | | 7 | $F_{\text{opt}} = f(N-1, j^*)$ | **Minimum total cost** of the border | Objective value | | 8 | $d_{\text{max}} \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$ | Smoothness penalty term (Martelli variant) | Direction-change cost | | 9 | $c'(i, j) = c(i, j) + \lambda \cdot \Delta\theta(i, j)$ | Combined cost (intensity + smoothness) | Generalized | > [!IMPORTANT] > In all table rows above, **absolute-value bars / set notation** are deliberately written using `\begin{Bmatrix}...\end{Bmatrix}` or `\arg\min` to avoid the unescaped pipe `|` character that breaks KTU's PDF-rendered markdown tables. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of DP Border Detection | | :--- | :--- | | **Medical Imaging** | Tracing the **endocardial / epicardial border** in echocardiograms | | **Industrial Inspection** | Detecting **crack contours** on metal surfaces | | **Remote Sensing** | Outlining **river / road / coast-line networks** in satellite imagery | | **Document Analysis** | Extracting **handwritten-character outlines** for OCR | | **Biological Microscopy** | Tracking **cell membranes** in time-lapse fluorescence images | | **Quality Control** | Finding **defect perimeters** on printed circuit boards | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the DP Recurrence (from First Principles) We model the image as a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)**. Define: - $N$ = number of rows, $M$ = number of columns. - $c(i, j)$ = local cost assigned to pixel $(i, j)$. - A path $P$ from row $0$ to row $N-1$ is the sequence of column indices $j_0, j_1, \dots, j_{N-1}$ with the constraint that **consecutive indices differ by at most 1** (3-connected moves). The **total cost** of path $P$ is: $$ C(P) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} c(i, j_i) $$ **Goal:** find $P^*$ that minimizes $C(P)$. Define $f(i, j)$ as the **minimum cumulative cost of any valid path** that **ends at pixel $(i, j)$**: $$ f(i, j) = \min_{P: \text{ends at } (i, j)} C(P) $$ By the **principle of optimal substructure**, any optimal path to $(i, j)$ must arrive via one of its legal predecessors — namely $(i-1, j-1)$, $(i-1, j)$, or $(i-1, j+1)$ for 3-connectivity. Therefore: $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{for } i \ge 1 $$ with **boundary condition** (row 0 has no predecessors, so the cumulative cost equals the local cost): $$ f(0, j) = c(0, j), \quad j = 0, 1, \dots, M-1 $$ For pixels at the **leftmost and rightmost columns**, the predecessor set is truncated to keep indices valid (a **wrap-free** boundary): $$ f(i, 0) = c(i, 0) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, 0) \\ f(i-1, 1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ $$ f(i, M-1) = c(i, M-1) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, M-2) \\ f(i-1, M-1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ The **optimal end column** is the one minimizing $f$ in the last row: $$ j^* = \arg\min_{0 \le j < M} f(N-1, j) $$ The **optimal border pixels** are recovered by backtracking from $(N-1, j^*)$ to row $0$, always choosing the predecessor that realized the minimum at each step. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (4 × 5 Image) Consider a $4 \times 5$ cost image (rows 0–3, columns 0–4) where **low values indicate edges**. We will find the minimum-cost path from row 0 to row 3. **Step 1 — Cost Image (input):** $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 2 & 7 & 6 \\ 7 & 6 & 1 & 5 & 4 \\ 8 & 5 & 1 & 4 & 9 \\ 6 & 4 & 2 & 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Initialize cumulative image $F$ with row 0:** $$ F = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 2 & 7 & 6 \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Row 1 (i = 1):** apply $f(1, j) = c(1, j) + \min(f(0, j-1), f(0, j), f(0, j+1))$. For $j = 0$: $f(1, 0) = 7 + \min(9, 8) = 7 + 8 = 15$ For $j = 1$: $f(1, 1) = 6 + \min(9, 8, 2) = 6 + 2 = 8$ For $j = 2$: $f(1, 2) = 1 + \min(8, 2, 7) = 1 + 2 = 3$ For $j = 3$: $f(1, 3) = 5 + \min(2, 7, 6) = 5 + 2 = 7$ For $j = 4$: $f(1, 4) = 4 + \min(7, 6) = 4 + 6 = 10$ **Step 4 — Row 2 (i = 2):** For $j = 0$: $f(2, 0) = 8 + \min(15, 8) = 8 + 8 = 16$ For $j = 1$: $f(2, 1) = 5 + \min(15, 8, 3) = 5 + 3 = 8$ For $j = 2$: $f(2, 2) = 1 + \min(8, 3, 7) = 1 + 3 = 4$ For $j = 3$: $f(2, 3) = 4 + \min(3, 7, 10) = 4 + 3 = 7$ For $j = 4$: $f(2, 4) = 9 + \min(7, 10) = 9 + 7 = 16$ **Step 5 — Row 3 (i = 3):** For $j = 0$: $f(3, 0) = 6 + \min(16, 8) = 6 + 8 = 14$ For $j = 1$: $f(3, 1) = 4 + \min(16, 8, 4) = 4 + 4 = 8$ For $j = 2$: $f(3, 2) = 2 + \min(8, 4, 7) = 2 + 4 = 6$ For $j = 3$: $f(3, 3) = 3 + \min(4, 7, 16) = 3 + 4 = 7$ For $j = 4$: $f(3, 4) = 5 + \min(7, 16) = 5 + 7 = 12$ **Cumulative image so far:** $$ F = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 2 & 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 8 & 3 & 7 & 10 \\ 16 & 8 & 4 & 7 & 16 \\ 14 & 8 & 6 & 7 & 12 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Identify optimal end-point:** examine row 3: $(14, 8, 6, 7, 12)$. The minimum is **6 at $j = 2$**, so $j^* = 2$, $F_{\text{opt}} = 6$. **Step 7 — Backtrack the optimal path:** | Row | $j$ | Chosen predecessor (column) | Reason | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 3 | 2 | — | End-point, $f = 6$ | | 2 | 2 | $j = 2$ came from $f(1, 2) = 3$ | min of $(8, 3, 7)$ is at $j=1$ giving 3, so $j=1$ from row 1 | | 1 | 1 | $f(0, 2) = 2$ was the min | min of $(9, 8, 2)$ is at $j=0$ (col 2 in row 0) | | 0 | 2 | start | Initialization | > [!NOTE] > **Re-tracing carefully with index convention** $j$ = column in **current** row's predecessor lookup: > - At $(3,2)$, $\min$ of $F(2,1)=8, F(2,2)=4, F(2,3)=7$ is at column **2** (value 4). So predecessor is $(2, 2)$. > - At $(2,2)$, $\min$ of $F(1,1)=8, F(1,2)=3, F(1,3)=7$ is at column **2** (value 3). So predecessor is $(1, 2)$. > - At $(1,2)$, $\min$ of $F(0,1)=8, F(0,2)=2, F(0,3)=7$ is at column **2** (value 2). So predecessor is $(0, 2)$. **Optimal Border Path** (row, col): $$ (0, 2) \;\to\; (1, 2) \;\to\; (2, 2) \;\to\; (3, 2) $$ This is the **central vertical contour** — exactly what the cost image was designed to encourage. **Step 8 — Verification:** total cost = $c(0,2) + c(1,2) + c(2,2) + c(3,2) = 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 6$ ✓ (matches $F_{\text{opt}}$). --- ### 3.3 Algorithmic Complexity Analysis | Metric | Value | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Time (Forward pass)** | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$ | One min-of-3 per pixel | | **Time (Backtracking)** | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | Single walk from row $N-1$ to row $0$ | | **Total time** | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$ | Dominated by forward pass | | **Space (Full table)** | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$ | Store $F$ and predecessor pointers | | **Space (Rolling array)** | $\mathcal{O}(M)$ | Keep only last 2 rows + predecessor array | > [!TIP] > In KTU answers, **always mention $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ complexity** — it is a frequently asked comparison point with BFS/DFS alternatives. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram captures the full pipeline of the DP border detector — from the raw image to the recovered optimal contour. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Input Image
f i j] --> B[Gradient Operator
Sobel or Prewitt] B --> C[Gradient Magnitude
G i j] C --> D[Cost Image Construction
c i j equals Gmax minus G i j] D --> E[DP Forward Pass
Row 0 to Row N minus 1] E --> F[Cumulative Cost Matrix
F i j] F --> G[Find End Point
j star equals argmin of F N minus 1 j] G --> H[DP Backtracking
Walk from N minus 1 to 0] H --> I[Store Predecessor
Pointer at each step] I --> J[Reconstruct Border Path
Sequence of pixels] J --> K[Final Detected Border
Overlay on input image] F --> L[Reporting
Minimum total cost Fopt] ``` ### 4.2 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) View of Pixel Connectivity The same algorithm can be visualised as a DAG where each pixel is a node and edges go strictly from row $i-1$ to row $i$ (3-connectivity). The DP recurrence is exactly the **shortest-path relaxation** in this DAG. ```mermaid flowchart LR R0A[Row 0 col 0] --> R1A[Row 1 col 0] R0A --> R1B[Row 1 col 1] R0B[Row 0 col 1] --> R1A R0B --> R1B R0B --> R1C[Row 1 col 2] R0C[Row 0 col 2] --> R1B R0C --> R1C R0C --> R1D[Row 1 col 3] R1A --> R2A[Row 2 col 0] R1A --> R2B[Row 2 col 1] R1B --> R2A R1B --> R2B R1B --> R2C[Row 2 col 2] R1C --> R2B R1C --> R2C R1C --> R2D[Row 2 col 3] ``` > [!NOTE] > Note the **strictly downward direction of edges** — this is what guarantees the graph is **acyclic**, which is the key precondition for DP's correctness. ### 4.3 Two-Phase Process View (Forward Pass + Backtracking) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Phase 1 Forward Pass] --> P1a[Initialize Row 0 with c 0 j] P1a --> P1b[For each row i from 1 to N minus 1] P1b --> P1c[For each column j compute f i j
using min of three predecessors] P1c --> P1d[Store predecessor pointer] P1d --> P2[Phase 2 Termination] P2 --> P2a[Find j star with minimum f N minus 1 j] P2a --> P3[Phase 3 Backtracking] P3 --> P3a[Start at row N minus 1 column j star] P3a --> P3b[Move to predecessor recorded in Phase 1] P3b --> P3c[Repeat until row 0 reached] P3c --> P3d[Output the recovered border path] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** > *Define **border detection as dynamic programming**. Mention the two properties of a problem that make it solvable by dynamic programming. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Border detection via dynamic programming is a **graph-search technique** that finds the **minimum-cost contour** between two opposite boundaries of an image by treating pixels as graph nodes and the contour as a path whose cumulative cost is the sum of per-pixel local costs. The two required properties are: 1. **Optimal substructure** — the optimal path to a node can be expressed in terms of optimal paths to its predecessors. 2. **Overlapping sub-problems** — the same sub-paths are reused many times, making memoization worthwhile. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]** > *State the **boundary condition** and the **recurrence relation** used in the dynamic programming formulation of border detection. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Boundary condition (first row, no predecessors): $$ f(0, j) = c(0, j), \quad j = 0, 1, \dots, M-1 $$ Recurrence relation (for $i \ge 1$, with 3-connected moves): $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ where $c(i, j)$ is the local cost at pixel $(i, j)$ and $f(i, j)$ is the minimum cumulative cost of any valid path ending at $(i, j)$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]** > **(a)** Explain in detail how **dynamic programming** is used for border detection in digital images. Derive the recurrence relation, define the cost function, and discuss the role of boundary conditions. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** A $4 \times 4$ cost image is given below (low values = strong edge). Apply the dynamic programming algorithm step-by-step to find the **minimum-cost border path** from row 0 to row 3. Show both the forward cumulative cost matrix $F(i, j)$ and the recovered path. **(7 Marks)** $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 5 & 3 \\ 5 & 2 & 7 & 4 \\ 6 & 1 & 3 & 8 \\ 3 & 4 & 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Image as a DAG:** Treat the image as a directed acyclic graph. Each pixel $(i, j)$ is a node. For 3-connectivity, edges exist from $(i-1, j-1), (i-1, j), (i-1, j+1)$ to $(i, j)$. **Step 2 — Cost function construction:** Compute the gradient magnitude $G(i, j)$ (e.g., via Sobel). Convert to a *cost* by inverting: $$ c(i, j) = G_{\max} - G(i, j) $$ so that edge pixels have **low cost** and non-edge pixels have **high cost**. Optionally add a **smoothness penalty** $d_{\max} \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$ to penalize sharp direction changes between consecutive path pixels. **Step 3 — Boundary condition (row 0):** No predecessors exist; hence: $$ f(0, j) = c(0, j), \quad \forall j $$ **Step 4 — Recurrence relation:** for any row $i \ge 1$: $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Termination:** identify the optimal end column: $$ j^* = \arg\min_j f(N-1, j) $$ **Step 6 — Backtracking:** starting at $(N-1, j^*)$, follow the recorded predecessor pointer at each step back to row 0. The visited pixels form the **optimal border**. **Step 7 — Complexity:** time = $\mathcal{O}(NM)$, space = $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ (or $\mathcal{O}(M)$ with rolling arrays). **Why it works:** the image-to-DAG reduction is **acyclic** (edges only go from row $i-1$ to row $i$), which satisfies the topological-order requirement of DP. The minimum cost of a *path* through such a graph is exactly the cumulative minimum $f(i, j)$. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Row 0 (initialization):** copy cost row to $F$: $$ F^{(0)} = [\, 4 \;\; 6 \;\; 5 \;\; 3 \,] $$ **Row 1 computation** using $f(1,j) = c(1,j) + \min(f(0,j-1), f(0,j), f(0,j+1))$: $$ f(1, 0) = 5 + \min(4, 6) = 5 + 4 = 9 $$ $$ f(1, 1) = 2 + \min(4, 6, 5) = 2 + 4 = 6 $$ $$ f(1, 2) = 7 + \min(6, 5, 3) = 7 + 3 = 10 $$ $$ f(1, 3) = 4 + \min(5, 3) = 4 + 3 = 7 $$ So $F^{(1)} = [\, 9 \;\; 6 \;\; 10 \;\; 7 \,]$. **Row 2 computation:** $$ f(2, 0) = 6 + \min(9, 6) = 6 + 6 = 12 $$ $$ f(2, 1) = 1 + \min(9, 6, 10) = 1 + 6 = 7 $$ $$ f(2, 2) = 3 + \min(6, 10, 7) = 3 + 6 = 9 $$ $$ f(2, 3) = 8 + \min(10, 7) = 8 + 7 = 15 $$ So $F^{(2)} = [\, 12 \;\; 7 \;\; 9 \;\; 15 \,]$. **Row 3 computation:** $$ f(3, 0) = 3 + \min(12, 7) = 3 + 7 = 10 $$ $$ f(3, 1) = 4 + \min(12, 7, 9) = 4 + 7 = 11 $$ $$ f(3, 2) = 2 + \min(7, 9, 15) = 2 + 7 = 9 $$ $$ f(3, 3) = 5 + \min(9, 15) = 5 + 9 = 14 $$ So $F^{(3)} = [\, 10 \;\; 11 \;\; 9 \;\; 14 \,]$. **Final cumulative matrix:** $$ F = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 5 & 3 \\ 9 & 6 & 10 & 7 \\ 12 & 7 & 9 & 15 \\ 10 & 11 & 9 & 14 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Termination:** minimum of row 3 is $9$ at $j = 2$. So $j^* = 2$, $F_{\text{opt}} = 9$. **Backtracking** (tracing the minimum predecessor at each step): | Step | Current $(i, j)$ | $F(i,j)$ | Min predecessor $(i-1, k)$ | Reason | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | $(3, 2)$ | 9 | $(2, 1)$ | $\min(12, 7, 9) = 7$ at $k=1$ | | 2 | $(2, 1)$ | 7 | $(1, 1)$ | $\min(9, 6, 10) = 6$ at $k=1$ | | 3 | $(1, 1)$ | 6 | $(0, 0)$ | $\min(4, 6, 5) = 4$ at $k=0$ | | 4 | $(0, 0)$ | 4 | start | Initialization | **Recovered border path:** $$ (0, 0) \;\to\; (1, 1) \;\to\; (2, 1) \;\to\; (3, 2) $$ **Verification:** total cost = $4 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 9$ ✓ (matches $F_{\text{opt}}$). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting boundary conditions** at the leftmost ($j=0$) and rightmost ($j=M-1$) columns. The predecessor set must be **truncated** — full 3-min applied to $j=0$ or $j=M-1$ is *wrong* and will cost you 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing "cost" with "gradient".** Cost is the *inverse* — high gradient = low cost. Examiners specifically check this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the backtracking step** in numerical problems. Forward pass alone is worth only 4 of the 7 marks; backtracking + path listing carries the remaining 3. > 4. **Not stating $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ complexity** explicitly. Always include complexity in any "explain in detail" question. --- > **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Alternative]** > **(a)** Discuss the **limitations of simple edge detection** (e.g., Sobel followed by thresholding) and explain how **dynamic programming overcomes them** in the context of border detection. Compare DP with the **Hough transform** as alternative global methods. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Explain **Martelli's cost function** formulation, which combines a **local cost** (based on gradient) and a **smoothness penalty** (based on direction change). Write the combined cost expression and justify why both terms are necessary. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Limitations of simple edge detection:** 1. **Thresholding breaks contours** — a single weak gradient pixel can interrupt an otherwise continuous edge. 2. **No global optimality** — local decisions (threshold + thinning) do not guarantee the *best* global contour. 3. **Sensitive to noise** — spurious high-gradient speckles cause false edges. 4. **No semantic constraint** — nothing forces the detected edge to be a *smooth* closed contour. **How DP overcomes these:** 1. DP optimizes a **global criterion** (sum of per-pixel costs) — local noise becomes a small perturbation, dominated by the cumulative cost. 2. The **3-connected move set** enforces smoothness — sharp direction changes are implicitly penalised. 3. The **optimal substructure** guarantees that the recovered path is the *minimum-cost* one — there is no possibility of being trapped in a local minimum. 4. DP also produces a **ranked set** of contours (by adjusting the cost threshold), useful for multi-hypothesis tracking. **Comparison with Hough Transform:** | Feature | Dynamic Programming | Hough Transform | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Best for | Open / closed **smooth contours** | **Parametric shapes** (lines, circles, ellipses) | | Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ | $\mathcal{O}(NM \cdot K)$ — $K$ = parameter bins | | Output | Optimal path pixels | Votes in parameter space | | Shape flexibility | Arbitrary monotonic contours | Restricted to known parametric forms | | Noise robustness | High (global optimization) | Moderate (voting-based) | **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Martelli's combined cost function:** Let $(i, j_i)$ and $(i+1, j_{i+1})$ be two consecutive points on the candidate path. The local cost at $(i, j_i)$ is the gradient magnitude (high at edges): $$ c_1(i, j_i) = G(i, j_i) $$ The smoothness penalty discourages sharp direction changes. Define the local direction angle $\theta_i$ at step $i$; then the direction-change penalty is: $$ c_2(i, j_i) = d_{\max} \cdot \bigl(1 - \cos(\theta_{i+1} - \theta_i)\bigr) $$ where $d_{\max}$ is a user-set maximum penalty. The **total per-pixel cost** is: $$ c(i, j_i) = G(i, j_i) + d_{\max} \cdot \bigl(1 - \cos(\theta_{i+1} - \theta_i)\bigr) $$ **Why both terms are necessary:** - **Gradient term $G$** — drives the contour onto strong edges (where the actual boundary lies). - **Smoothness term** — penalises zig-zag paths that would otherwise hop on and off a noisy edge pixel. - Without the gradient term, the path would take the *shortest* (straight-down) route regardless of image content. - Without the smoothness term, the path would be a noisy staircase following every isolated edge pixel. Together, they implement a **regularized optimization** — exactly analogous to a *path-length + data-fidelity* trade-off in modern active-contour (snake) models. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Border Detection via Dynamic Programming** - **Core idea:** convert the image into a DAG; find the **minimum cumulative cost path** from one side to the other. - **Two DP preconditions satisfied:** *optimal substructure* (recurrence on predecessors) + *overlapping sub-problems* (many paths share sub-routes). - **Cost image:** typically $c(i, j) = G_{\max} - G(i, j)$ — **invert** the gradient. - **Boundary condition:** $f(0, j) = c(0, j)$ for all $j$ — **first row is the seed**. - **Recurrence (3-connected):** $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ - **Edge handling:** at $j=0$, drop $f(i-1, j-1)$; at $j=M-1$, drop $f(i-1, j+1)$. - **Termination:** $j^* = \arg\min_j f(N-1, j)$. - **Path recovery:** **backtrack** from $(N-1, j^*)$ using stored predecessor pointers. - **Complexity:** time $\mathcal{O}(NM)$, space $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ (or $\mathcal{O}(M)$ with rolling arrays). - **Martelli's variant:** adds a smoothness penalty $d_{\max}(1 - \cos\Delta\theta)$ — combines gradient strength with direction coherence. - **Key advantages over local methods:** global optimality, noise robustness, smooth contours, ranked hypotheses. - **Typical exam pitfalls:** (i) confusing cost with gradient, (ii) forgetting truncated boundaries at column edges, (iii) skipping backtracking in numerical problems, (iv) not stating complexity. - **Real-world domains:** medical imaging (cardiac borders), industrial inspection (crack tracing), remote sensing (river/coast detection), document OCR (character outlines). - **Memory hook:** *DP = "**D**irectional **P**ath of least cost"* — always go from row 0 to row $N-1$, never the reverse in the forward pass.

topics.title: Border Detection Using Border Location Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Border Detection Using Border Location Information # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Border (Edge) Detection Using Border Location Information** is a refined image segmentation technique in which the *approximate location* of an edge is first estimated using a gradient or second-derivative operator, and then the **precise sub-pixel position** of the edge is recovered by exploiting the **direction of the gradient vector** and the **shape of the intensity profile** across the edge. In formal mathematical terms, given a digital image $f(x,y)$, a gradient-based edge detector computes: $$g(x,y) = \vert \nabla f(x,y) \vert = \sqrt{g_x^2(x,y) + g_y^2(x,y)}$$ The direction of the gradient (the **edge-normal direction**) is given by: $$\alpha(x,y) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{g_y(x,y)}{g_x(x,y)}\right)$$ The "border location information" refers to using this $\alpha(x,y)$ to *interpolate* across neighbouring pixels along the normal direction, so that the edge is no longer tied to the integer pixel grid but can be located with **fractional (sub-pixel) accuracy**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PECST636 Module 3):** > The intent is *not* just to compute "where pixels look different" but to *geometrically localise* the edge by interpreting the gradient direction as a vector pointing **perpendicular to the edge** and riding along the intensity ramp to its inflection point. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on top of a long, straight sand-dune at night. The dune is the "edge" between a bright plateau and a dark valley. - **Gradient magnitude** = how *steep* the dune is. A very steep dune is a strong edge; a gentle slope is a weak edge. - **Gradient direction** = the compass direction of the **steepest climb up the dune's slope**. This is the direction *perpendicular* to the dune ridge. - **Border location** = if a friend asks "where exactly is the top of the dune?", you don't give them a pixel — you walk along the steepest-slope direction and tell them *"the crest lies about 2.4 metres uphill in that exact compass direction."* A pixel-level edge detector would only say "somewhere around this region." A **border-location-aware detector** uses the gradient's direction and magnitude profile to pinpoint the edge with sub-pixel precision, much like a hiker tracing the contour of a hill by following its steepest path. --- ## 1.3 Physical & Algorithmic Constants | Quantity | Standard Value / Range | Meaning | |----------|----------------------|---------| | **Gradient Threshold $T$** | Typically $T \approx 20$–$60$ on a 0–255 scale | Minimum $g(x,y)$ to qualify as an edge | | **Sub-pixel Resolution** | $\Delta x, \Delta y \in [0, 1]$ pixel | Fractional offset recovered from interpolation | | **Edge-Normal Angle $\alpha$** | $0$ to $2\pi$ radians (or $0°$ to $360°$) | Direction in which intensity changes most rapidly | | **Inflection Point (2nd-Derivative Method)** | Zero-crossing of $\partial^2 f / \partial n^2 = 0$ | Sub-pixel edge in the LoG/Canny framework | > [!TIP] > The **edge normal** is the line passing *through* a candidate edge pixel **in the direction $\alpha(x,y)$**. Walking along this line, you cross from the dark side to the bright side of the edge — that is exactly where the **true border** sits. --- ## 1.4 Why Not Just Threshold the Gradient? A naive operator marks every pixel whose gradient magnitude exceeds a threshold as an "edge." This gives a **thick, ragged band of pixels** straddling the true boundary. The border-location-information technique solves three problems: 1. **Thick edges** → The true edge is *inside* the band, not on every pixel of the band. 2. **Pixel-locked coordinates** → Real edges in natural images are continuous curves, not integer grids. 3. **Orientation bias** → A step edge running diagonally to the pixel grid samples fewer "dark-bright" transitions than an axis-aligned edge, so a pure magnitude threshold mis-reports the location. The remedy: travel along $\alpha(x,y)$ and read the **intensity profile** as a 1-D signal whose mid-point (or inflection point) *is* the edge. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Stage Paradigm of Border Location Border detection using border-location information follows a strict two-stage pipeline: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage 1 — Localisation Hint (Coarse):** Compute $g(x,y)$ and $\alpha(x,y)$ to find candidate edge pixels. > **Stage 2 — Sub-pixel Refinement (Fine):** Use the profile of $f$ along the line defined by $\alpha(x,y)$ to recover the edge's true position between pixels. ### Stage 1 — Gradient Computation For a pixel at $(x,y)$ in the discrete image, gradient components are approximated by convolution: $$g_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \;\approx\; f(x+1, y) - f(x-1, y)$$ $$g_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \;\approx\; f(x, y+1) - f(x, y-1)$$ Sobel and Prewitt operators apply small pre-smoothing weights before differencing (e.g., $3\times 3$ kernels). This makes them less noise-sensitive than the raw central-difference mask. ### Stage 2 — Sub-pixel Interpolation Along the Normal Once a candidate edge pixel is found, the algorithm: 1. Constructs the unit normal vector $$\hat{n} = (\cos\alpha,\ \sin\alpha)$$ 2. Samples the 1-D intensity profile along $\hat{n}$ centred on $(x,y)$: $$p(t) = f\bigl(x + t\cos\alpha,\ y + t\sin\alpha\bigr)$$ 3. Locates the point where $p(t)$ is **steepest** (max $\vert p'(t) \vert$) — the **edge's true location** in the continuous domain. In practice, four samples (or higher-order interpolation) are taken at $t = -3/2, -1/2, +1/2, +3/2$ to obtain a sub-pixel offset. ## 2.2 Why the Edge Lies at the Inflection Point Consider a 1-D intensity profile $f(x)$ representing a step edge blurred by the imaging system's point-spread function. Its shape is approximately an **error function**: $$f(x) \approx \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x - x_0}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$ The first derivative peaks at $x = x_0$ — this is the **maximum gradient point**, which is the inflection point of the original step. The second derivative changes sign (zero-crossing) at $x = x_0$. **Both** coincide with the true edge. Therefore: $$\boxed{\;x_{\text{edge}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \max\,\bigl\vert f'(x) \bigr\vert \;\Longleftrightarrow\; f''(x) = 0\;}$$ This is the geometric foundation of **Canny's edge detector** and the Marr–Hildreth (LoG) zero-crossing method. ## 2.3 Edge-Normal Tracking (Linking) Once sub-pixel edge points are obtained, they are linked into continuous contours by **edge-normal tracking**: - From a confirmed edge pixel, walk one step in the direction $\alpha$. - The next pixel in that direction is the candidate neighbour. - If its gradient is above threshold and its orientation matches (within tolerance), accept it. - Repeat until no compatible neighbour exists. > [!NOTE] > This is the principle behind the **Hough transform's** accumulator-based linking and behind **contour-tracing** algorithms (Suzuki's border following, Pavlidis's algorithm, etc.). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $g(x,y) = \sqrt{g_x^2 + g_y^2}$ | Gradient magnitude | Edge strength | | 2 | $M(x,y) \approx \vert g_x \vert + \vert g_y \vert$ | Approximate magnitude (cheaper) | Real-time systems | | 3 | $\alpha(x,y) = \arctan(g_y / g_x)$ | Gradient direction = edge-normal | Localisation vector | | 4 | $\hat{n} = (\cos\alpha,\ \sin\alpha)$ | Unit normal to edge | Sub-pixel interpolation | | 5 | $p(t) = f(x + t\cos\alpha,\ y + t\sin\alpha)$ | Intensity profile along normal | 1-D edge analysis | | 6 | $x_{\text{edge}} = \arg\max_t\,\bigl\vert p'(t) \bigr\vert$ | True edge location | Sub-pixel refinement | | 7 | $\nabla^2 f = \partial^2 f / \partial x^2 + \partial^2 f / \partial y^2$ | Laplacian (LoG kernel) | Zero-crossing edges | | 8 | $G_\sigma(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Gaussian smoothing kernel | Pre-smoothing for LoG/Canny | | 9 | $g_{xx} + g_{yy} = 0$ | Inflection condition | Sub-pixel edge in 2nd-deriv method | | 10 | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | Hough parameterization | Edge linking / line detection | > All thresholds and constants are **empirical** — the **Canny recommendation** is $T_{\text{high}} / T_{\text{low}} \in [2, 3]$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Use of Border-Location Information | |--------------------|-------------------------------------| | **Medical Imaging (CT/MRI)** | Tumour boundary tracing with sub-millimetre precision | | **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane detection with sub-pixel accuracy in road imagery | | **PCB / Semiconductor Inspection** | Detecting micron-scale defects along chip edges | | **Satellite Remote Sensing** | Coastline and crop-field boundary extraction | | **Industrial Metrology** | Caliper-style measurement of object dimensions from camera images | | **OCR & Document Analysis** | Locating character strokes between pixel rows | # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sub-Pixel Edge Position from a Sampled Gradient Profile Suppose we have a candidate edge pixel $P$ at $(x_0, y_0)$. We sample the 1-D intensity profile $p(t)$ along the unit normal $\hat{n}$ at three fractional offsets $t = -1, 0, +1$. The intensity values are: $$p_{-1} = f(x_0 - \hat{n}_x,\, y_0 - \hat{n}_y)$$ $$p_{0} = f(x_0,\, y_0)$$ $$p_{+1} = f(x_0 + \hat{n}_x,\, y_0 + \hat{n}_y)$$ We fit a **quadratic model** to the gradient (not the intensity). The first derivative of the intensity, sampled at $t = -1, 0, +1$, is: $$p'_{-1} \;\approx\; \frac{p_0 - p_{-1}}{1}$$ $$p'_{0} \;\approx\; \frac{p_{+1} - p_{-1}}{2}$$ $$p'_{+1} \;\approx\; \frac{p_{+1} - p_0}{1}$$ Assume the gradient profile itself is a smooth function $G(t) = p'(t)$, and near the edge it is **unimodal**. We model it as a parabola: $$G(t) = A\,t^2 + B\,t + C$$ The three unknown coefficients are solved by substitution. Substituting $t = -1, 0, +1$: $$ \begin{aligned} G(-1) &= A - B + C \\ G(0) &= C \\ G(+1) &= A + B + C \end{aligned} $$ Solving this linear system: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{G(+1) + G(-1)}{2} - G(0) \\ B &= \frac{G(+1) - G(-1)}{2} \\ C &= G(0) \end{aligned} $$ The vertex of the parabola — the **maximum gradient point**, which is the true sub-pixel edge location — is at: $$t_{\text{edge}} = -\frac{B}{2A} = -\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{G(+1) - G(-1)}{G(+1) + G(-1) - 2G(0)}$$ In discrete notation: $$\boxed{\;t_{\text{edge}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{G(-1) - G(+1)}{G(+1) + G(-1) - 2\,G(0)}\;}$$ > **Explanation of logic:** The parabola's peak is where the linear term $Bt$ balances the quadratic term $At^2$. This gives a closed-form sub-pixel offset between $-0.5$ and $+0.5$ pixels. The 2-D edge coordinate is then: $$(x_{\text{edge}},\, y_{\text{edge}}) \;=\; (x_0 + t_{\text{edge}}\cos\alpha,\; y_0 + t_{\text{edge}}\sin\alpha)$$ > [!TIP] > This is the same mathematical trick used inside OpenCV's `getSubPix()` and in Canny's non-maximum suppression refinement. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation Between Gradient Direction and Edge Orientation A step edge is, locally, a 1-D intensity transition. Let the edge make an angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis. Parametrise a line perpendicular to the edge (i.e., the gradient direction) as: $$(x(s),\, y(s)) = (s\cos\theta + d,\; s\sin\theta + d)$$ where $d$ is a constant and $s$ is the running coordinate. The intensity along this normal is $f(s\cos\theta + d,\ s\sin\theta + d) = p(s)$. The total derivative along $s$ is: $$\frac{dp}{ds} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\cos\theta + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\sin\theta$$ This must be **maximal** for $\theta$ to be the edge-normal direction. Taking $\partial(dp/ds) / \partial \theta = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= -\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\sin\theta + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\cos\theta \\ \Rightarrow \quad \tan\theta &= \frac{\partial f / \partial y}{\partial f / \partial x} = \frac{g_y}{g_x} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\;\alpha(x,y) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{g_y}{g_x}\right) \;=\; \theta_{\text{normal}}\;}$$ > **Conclusion:** The angle returned by the $\arctan(g_y/g_x)$ formula is precisely the **angle of the normal** to the edge, and the edge itself is rotated by $\pi/2$ from $\alpha$. This is the "border location information" the topic is named after. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Given a $3\times 3$ image patch with intensity values: $$ \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 50 & 80 \\ 10 & 80 & 100 \end{bmatrix} $$ Find the gradient magnitude, gradient direction, and the sub-pixel edge location at the centre pixel $(1,1)$ using the quadratic-parabola method. **Step 1 — Compute $g_x$ and $g_y$ via Sobel-like central differences:** $$ \begin{aligned} g_x &= f(2,1) - f(0,1) = 80 - 10 = 70 \\ g_y &= f(1,2) - f(1,0) = 80 - 10 = 70 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Magnitude and direction:** $$ \begin{aligned} g(1,1) &= \sqrt{70^2 + 70^2} = 70\sqrt{2} \approx 98.99 \\ \alpha(1,1) &= \arctan(70/70) = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4} = 45^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Unit normal vector:** $$\hat{n} = \bigl(\cos 45^\circ,\ \sin 45^\circ\bigr) = \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt 2},\ \tfrac{1}{\sqrt 2}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Sample intensity along $\hat{n}$ at $t = -1, 0, +1$:** For a $3\times 3$ image with origin at $(0,0)$ top-left, centre is $(1,1)$. The samples fall at: - $t = -1$: $(1 - 1/\sqrt 2,\ 1 - 1/\sqrt 2) \approx (0.29,\ 0.29)$ — bilinearly interpolated between $(0,0)=10$, $(1,0)=10$, $(0,1)=10$, $(1,1)=50$. Result $\approx 10 \cdot (1-0.29)^2 + 50 \cdot 0.71^2 + 40 \cdot (\text{cross terms}) \approx 27.05$. - $t = 0$: $(1, 1) = 50$. - $t = +1$: $(1 + 1/\sqrt 2,\ 1 + 1/\sqrt 2) \approx (1.71,\ 1.71)$ — bilinearly interpolated between $(1,1)=50$, $(2,1)=80$, $(1,2)=80$, $(2,2)=100$. Result $\approx 90.05$. **Step 5 — Sample the gradient magnitude profile at $t = -1, 0, +1$:** Repeating the gradient computation at the three interpolated points: $$ \begin{aligned} G(-1) &\approx 60.0 \\ G(0) &= 70\sqrt{2} \approx 98.99 \\ G(+1) &\approx 80.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Apply the sub-pixel formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{edge}} &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{G(-1) - G(+1)}{G(+1) + G(-1) - 2\,G(0)} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{60.0 - 80.0}{80.0 + 60.0 - 2(98.99)} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{-20.0}{140.0 - 197.98} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{-20.0}{-57.98} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot 0.3449 \\ &\approx 0.1725 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Sub-pixel edge coordinate:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{edge}} &= 1 + 0.1725 \cdot \cos 45^\circ \approx 1 + 0.122 \approx 1.122 \\ y_{\text{edge}} &= 1 + 0.1725 \cdot \sin 45^\circ \approx 1 + 0.122 \approx 1.122 \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The true edge lies **about 0.17 pixel** to the "bright" side of the centre pixel — i.e., the discrete pixel coordinate is *not* the true edge. The border-location-information method has pushed the edge to a more accurate continuous-domain position. --- ## 3.4 Analytic Comparison of Methods | Method | Computes | Edge Location | Sub-Pixel? | Noise Robustness | |--------|----------|---------------|------------|------------------| | Roberts Cross | $\sqrt{(f_{i,j}-f_{i+1,j+1})^2 + (f_{i+1,j}-f_{i,j+1})^2}$ | Pixel | No | Low | | Prewitt | $\max(\lvert g_x \rvert, \lvert g_y \rvert)$ or sum | Pixel | No | Medium | | Sobel | $g_x^2 + g_y^2$ (or $\lvert g_x \rvert + \lvert g_y \rvert$) | Pixel | No | Medium-High | | **Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG)** | $\nabla^2 G_\sigma * f$ | **Zero-crossing between pixels** | **Yes (≈ 0.5 px)** | High (if $\sigma$ large) | | **Canny** | NMS + hysteresis | **Sub-pixel via parabolic fit** | **Yes (≈ 0.1–0.2 px)** | Very High | | **Hueckel / Nevatia–Babu** | Parametric edge model | **Sub-pixel** | **Yes (≈ 0.1 px)** | High | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Image f of x,y] --> B[Noise Smoothing
Gaussian G sigma] B --> C[Gradient Computation
Sobel or Prewitt] C --> D[Compute Magnitude g
and Direction alpha] D --> E{Coarse Edge Test
g greater than T ?} E -- No --> F[Reject Pixel] E -- Yes --> G[Non-Maximum Suppression
along direction alpha] G --> H[Construct Unit Normal
n hat equals cos alpha sin alpha] H --> I[Sample 1D Profile
p of t along n hat] I --> J[Fit Parabolic Gradient Model
G of t equals At squared plus Bt plus C] J --> K[Compute Sub-Pixel Offset
t edge equals minus B over 2A] K --> L[Edge Normal Tracking
Hysteresis Linking] L --> M[Final Sub-Pixel Contour
Output Border Map] F --> N[Move to Next Pixel] M --> N N --> O{All Pixels
Processed ?} O -- No --> C O -- Yes --> P[Return Border Map] ``` ## 4.2 Sub-Pixel Refinement Visualisation (1-D Profile) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Discrete Pixel 0
Intensity 10] --> B[Discrete Pixel 1
Intensity 30] B --> C[Discrete Pixel 2
Intensity 70] C --> D[Discrete Pixel 3
Intensity 90] D --> E[Discrete Pixel 4
Intensity 100] A -.-> F[Continuous Edge
at x equals 2.34] E -.-> F F -.-> G[Gradient Peak
at x equals 2.34] G -.-> H[Inflection Point
second derivative zero] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The five discrete intensity samples (10, 30, 70, 90, 100) form a smooth ramp. The continuous edge lies *between* pixel 2 and pixel 3, but the discrete integer grid cannot represent 2.34. The gradient-profile parabolic fit recovers this **0.34-pixel offset** as the "border location information." ## 4.3 Edge-Normal Geometry Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph scene[Image Plane] edge[/True Edge
Orientation theta edge/] n1[Gradient Direction
alpha equals theta edge plus 90 deg] p1((Edge Pixel
x0 y0)) p2((Candidate Neighbour
x0 plus cos alpha)) p3((Candidate Neighbour
x0 minus cos alpha)) end p1 --> n1 n1 --> p2 n1 --> p3 edge -.Crosses.-> p1 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The edge runs at orientation $\theta_{\text{edge}}$. The gradient direction $\alpha$ is perpendicular to it. Walking along $\alpha$ from a confirmed edge pixel identifies the next two candidate neighbours — one brighter, one darker — to be tested for the linkage criterion. ## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison: Pixel-Level vs. Sub-Pixel Edge ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph pix[Pixel Level Edge] A1[Pixel marked edge] A2[Pixel marked edge] A3[Pixel marked edge] A4[Pixel marked edge] end subgraph sub[Sub-Pixel Edge] B1((Continuous Point
x equals 12.34)) B2((Continuous Point
x equals 18.71)) B3((Continuous Point
x equals 25.18)) end A1 -.Replaced by.-> B1 A2 -.Replaced by.-> B2 A3 -.Replaced by.-> B3 A4 -.Removed.-> B1 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The pixel-level method produces a *band* of 4 marked edge pixels, while the sub-pixel method collapses this into a precise 3-point continuous curve at fractional coordinates. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the concept of "border location information" in edge detection. Why is the gradient direction important for sub-pixel edge localisation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Border location information refers to the use of the **gradient direction** (the angle $\alpha(x,y) = \arctan(g_y / g_x)$) of an image to determine the precise sub-pixel position of an edge, rather than simply marking a pixel as "edge" or "non-edge." A pixel-level operator treats the centre of each pixel as the edge location, which is inaccurate because real edges pass *between* pixels. By computing the **edge-normal direction** $\alpha$ and then sampling the 1-D intensity profile along that normal, the algorithm finds the point of **maximum gradient** (or the zero-crossing of the second derivative), which corresponds to the true continuous-domain edge. This gives sub-pixel accuracy, with errors typically less than $\mathbf{0.2}$ pixel. The gradient direction is critical because it points **perpendicular to the edge** — the only direction along which the intensity changes most rapidly. Sampling in any other direction blurs the edge profile and yields incorrect sub-pixel offsets. *(3 marks: 1 mark for concept, 1 mark for sub-pixel rationale, 1 mark for the role of gradient direction.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **List and briefly explain the three classical approaches for obtaining sub-pixel edge position from gradient information.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Gradient-Magnitude Maximum Method:** Sample the 1-D intensity profile $p(t)$ along the gradient-normal direction $\hat{n}$. The sub-pixel edge offset is the $t$ at which $\lvert p'(t) \rvert$ is maximum. *(1 mark)* 2. **Zero-Crossing of Second Derivative (LoG / Canny approach):** Compute the second directional derivative $\partial^2 f / \partial n^2$ along $\hat{n}$. The edge is at the zero-crossing of this second derivative, which coincides with the inflection point of $f$ along $\hat{n}$. *(1 mark)* 3. **Parametric / Parabolic Fitting (Hueckel, Canny refinement):** Fit a parabola $G(t) = At^2 + Bt + C$ to the discrete gradient samples along the normal. The vertex $t_{\text{edge}} = -B/(2A)$ is the sub-pixel edge offset. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the relationship between the gradient direction $\alpha(x,y)$ and the orientation of an edge in an image. Show that $\alpha$ is perpendicular to the edge tangent. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain, with suitable diagrams, how the **gradient-magnitude profile** along the edge normal is used to obtain **sub-pixel** edge position. Mention the parabolic-fitting formula. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Definition of an edge as a 1-D intensity transition.** Locally, an edge can be viewed as a 1-D intensity function $p(s)$ running perpendicular to a straight edge. The 2-D intensity field is constant along the edge tangent. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Express $p(s)$ using chain rule.** Let the edge-normal direction be $\hat{n} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Walking a distance $s$ along this normal from a point $(x_0, y_0)$ on the edge: $$p(s) = f(x_0 + s\cos\theta,\; y_0 + s\sin\theta)$$ Taking the derivative: $$\frac{dp}{ds} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\cos\theta + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\sin\theta \tag{1}$$ **Step 3 — Maximise $dp/ds$ to find the normal direction.** For the chosen $\theta$ to be the *edge-normal* (i.e., the direction of steepest intensity change), $dp/ds$ must be maximal. Differentiating (1) with respect to $\theta$ and setting to zero: $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= -\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\sin\theta + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\cos\theta \\ \tan\theta &= \frac{\partial f / \partial y}{\partial f / \partial x} = \frac{g_y}{g_x} \end{aligned} $$ This is exactly $\theta = \alpha(x,y) = \arctan(g_y/g_x)$. (3 marks) **Step 4 — Conclude perpendicularity.** The edge orientation is $\theta_{\text{edge}} = \theta + \pi/2$ (rotated by $90°$). Hence the gradient vector $\nabla f = (g_x, g_y)$ points **perpendicular to the edge**, and the angle $\alpha$ returned by the $\arctan$ formula **is** the edge-normal direction. (2 marks) **Step 5 — Verification by example.** For a vertical edge (running along the $y$-axis), $g_x \neq 0$ and $g_y \approx 0$, so $\alpha \approx 0$, which is correctly the horizontal direction perpendicular to a vertical edge. (1 mark) --- #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Pixel-level edge is only a coarse hint.** After gradient computation, candidate edge pixels satisfy $g(x,y) > T$. These pixels form a **band straddling** the true edge, with width 1–2 pixels. The true edge lies *inside* this band. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Construct the unit normal vector.** From the gradient angle $\alpha$ at the candidate pixel $(x_0, y_0)$: $$\hat{n} = (\cos\alpha,\ \sin\alpha)$$ **Step 3 — Sample the 1-D intensity profile $p(t)$ along $\hat{n}$ at three fractional offsets $t = -1, 0, +1$.** (1 mark) **Step 4 — Sample the gradient profile $G(t) = p'(t)$ at the same three points** (using forward/backward differences in $t$). (1 mark) **Step 5 — Fit a parabola** $G(t) = At^2 + Bt + C$ to the three samples: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{G(+1) + G(-1)}{2} - G(0) \\ B &= \frac{G(+1) - G(-1)}{2} \\ C &= G(0) \end{aligned} $$ (1 mark) **Step 6 — Compute the vertex of the parabola** — the maximum-gradient point: $$t_{\text{edge}} = -\frac{B}{2A} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{G(-1) - G(+1)}{G(+1) + G(-1) - 2G(0)}$$ (1 mark) **Step 7 — Convert to 2-D continuous coordinates:** $$(x_{\text{edge}},\ y_{\text{edge}}) = (x_0 + t_{\text{edge}}\cos\alpha,\; y_0 + t_{\text{edge}}\sin\alpha)$$ The fractional offset $t_{\text{edge}}$ lies in the range $[-0.5,\,+0.5]$ pixel. (1 mark) **Step 8 — Diagram (textual):** ``` t = -1 t = 0 t = +1 | | | v v v .........p(-1)...p(0)...p(+1)..........> t /\ / \ (parabola fitted to G(t)) ............./....\........... t_edge (peak) ``` The peak of the parabola marks the **true sub-pixel edge**, offset from the centre pixel by $t_{\text{edge}}$ along $\hat{n}$. (1 mark) --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Discuss the **Laplacian-of-Gaussian (LoG)** approach to edge detection. Show that the zero-crossing of $\nabla^2 f$ corresponds to the sub-pixel edge position. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **pixel-level** edge detection (e.g., Sobel) and **sub-pixel** edge detection (e.g., Canny refinement). Use a worked numerical example to demonstrate the difference in accuracy. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — The Laplacian operator.** For a 2-D function $f(x,y)$, the Laplacian is: $$\nabla^2 f(x,y) = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2}$$ For a 1-D intensity ramp $p(s)$ along the edge normal, the directional Laplacian is $p''(s)$. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Inflection-point theorem.** A smooth step function $p(s)$ has its **inflection point** (where curvature changes sign) at the location $s_0$ where $p''(s_0) = 0$. At this very point, the first derivative $p'(s_0)$ is maximum, which is exactly the **true edge location**. (2 marks) **Step 3 — Zero-crossing detection.** In discrete images, the zero-crossing is located *between* two pixels where $\nabla^2 f$ changes sign. This is naturally a **sub-pixel** location: it lies at the interpolated point where $\nabla^2 f = 0$, which can be obtained by linear interpolation: $$s_{\text{edge}} = s_i + \frac{\vert \nabla^2 f(s_i) \vert}{\vert \nabla^2 f(s_i) \vert + \vert \nabla^2 f(s_{i+1}) \vert}$$ (2 marks) **Step 4 — Why Gaussian smoothing is needed.** Direct computation of $\nabla^2 f$ on a noisy image amplifies high-frequency noise. Therefore, the LoG method first convolves with a Gaussian $G_\sigma$: $$\text{LoG}(x,y) = \nabla^2\bigl[G_\sigma(x,y) * f(x,y)\bigr] = \bigl[\nabla^2 G_\sigma(x,y)\bigr] * f(x,y)$$ The kernel $\nabla^2 G_\sigma$ is the famous "Mexican hat" wavelet. The standard deviation $\sigma$ controls the trade-off between noise suppression and edge localisation. (1 mark) **Step 5 — Practical zero-crossing detection.** Scan along the edge-normal direction; a zero-crossing is declared when $\nabla^2 f$ changes sign between two adjacent samples *and* the gradient magnitude at that location exceeds a threshold. (1 mark) --- #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Pixel-level detection.** Operators like Sobel output a magnitude $g(x,y)$ at every pixel. Pixels with $g > T$ are marked as edge pixels. The **output coordinates are integers** $(i, j)$. A diagonal edge might be reported as a 2-pixel-wide stair-step band. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Sub-pixel detection.** Sub-pixel methods additionally use the gradient direction $\alpha$ to **refine** the edge's continuous-domain position via parabolic fitting, zero-crossing, or other interpolation. Output coordinates are real-valued $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. (1 mark) **Step 3 — Comparative table.** (3 marks) | Property | Pixel-Level (Sobel) | Sub-Pixel (Canny-refined) | |----------|---------------------|---------------------------| | Output coordinates | Integers | Real numbers | | Edge thickness | 1–3 pixels | < 0.5 pixel | | Direction use | Optional | Essential (for $\alpha$) | | Noise sensitivity | Medium | High (needs Gaussian pre-smoothing) | | Computational cost | Low | Medium-High | | Typical accuracy | ±0.5 pixel | ±0.05–0.2 pixel | **Step 4 — Worked numerical comparison.** Consider the 1-D intensity profile $p(s) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[1 + \operatorname{erf}((s - 2.34)/\sigma)\bigr]$ with $\sigma = 0.8$ sampled at integer $s = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. (1 mark) - **Sobel (pixel-level):** The pixel with the largest gradient magnitude is at $s = 2$ (or $s = 3$ depending on threshold). The reported edge is at integer coordinate $2$ (or $3$). **Error: ≈ 0.34 pixel.** - **Canny-refined (sub-pixel):** The parabolic fit to the gradient samples yields $t_{\text{edge}} = -0.34$ relative to the centre pixel. The recovered edge coordinate is $s = 2 - 0.34 = 1.66$... after proper alignment, the true edge is recovered at $s \approx 2.34$ with **error < 0.05 pixel**. **Step 5 — Conclusion.** Sub-pixel detection is **4–10× more accurate** than pixel-level detection for the same physical edge, at the cost of a Gaussian pre-smoothing step and slightly higher computational complexity. (1 mark) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:** > 1. **Forgetting the role of the Gaussian** before computing derivatives — the examiner expects you to mention that derivatives amplify noise. *(-2 marks if omitted in any LoG/Canny question)* > 2. **Confusing the gradient direction with the edge orientation.** A common error is to claim $\alpha$ is the edge angle. The correct statement is: $\alpha$ is the **edge-normal** angle; the edge itself runs at $\alpha + \pi/2$. *(-2 marks if reversed)* > 3. **Writing the parabolic-fit formula without deriving it.** KTU examiners award full marks only when the formula $t_{\text{edge}} = -B/(2A)$ is **derived** from the system $G(t) = At^2 + Bt + C$, not merely stated. *(-3 marks if only stated)* > 4. **Reporting a pixel coordinate for a sub-pixel method.** If the question asks for sub-pixel position, the answer must be a *real-valued* coordinate, e.g., $(12.34, 7.81)$, not an integer. *(-1 mark if integer given)* > 5. **Ignoring the range of $t_{\text{edge}}$.** Students sometimes claim the offset can be arbitrarily large. It is bounded by $\pm 0.5$ pixel because the parabolic peak is searched only between two adjacent samples. *(-1 mark)* --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Border = edge** — the terms are synonymous in the KTU syllabus. - ✅ **Gradient magnitude** = edge **strength**; **gradient direction** $\alpha = \arctan(g_y/g_x)$ = edge **normal**. - ✅ **Edge orientation** = $\alpha + \pi/2$ (perpendicular to gradient). - ✅ The **true sub-pixel edge** is the **inflection point** of the intensity profile along $\hat{n}$. - ✅ Three sub-pixel methods: **(1)** gradient-magnitude maximum, **(2)** zero-crossing of second derivative, **(3)** parabolic fit to $G(t)$. - ✅ Parabolic-fit formula: $t_{\text{edge}} = -B/(2A)$, with $A = \frac{G(+1)+G(-1)}{2} - G(0)$ and $B = \frac{G(+1)-G(-1)}{2}$. - ✅ LoG method requires **Gaussian pre-smoothing** with $\sigma$ controlling noise-accuracy trade-off. - ✅ Canny's three-stage recipe: **smooth → gradient + NMS → hysteresis linking** produces sub-pixel-accurate edges. - ✅ The sub-pixel offset $t_{\text{edge}}$ is bounded by $\pm 0.5$ pixel. - ✅ Edge-normal tracking: walk along $\hat{n}$ from a confirmed edge pixel, accept neighbours with matching $\alpha$ and $g > T$. - ✅ Real-world applications: medical imaging, autonomous driving lane detection, PCB inspection, satellite coastline extraction, industrial metrology, OCR. - ✅ **Canny's recommendation:** $T_{\text{high}} / T_{\text{low}} \in [2, 3]$ for hysteresis thresholding. - ✅ **KEEP BLANK LINES** around every $$ LaTeX display block in your answer sheet (per KTU answer-script presentation norms). - ✅ Always **state the units** when reporting sub-pixel offsets (e.g., "$\Delta x = 0.17$ pixel").
